title: Heart Racing Out of Control
subtitle: Atrial Fibrillation with RVR

patient.name: Gerald Marsh
patient.chiefComplaint: Palpitations and shortness of breath for 6 hours
medications: Aspirin 81mg OD — recently stopped anticoagulation against medical advice
patient.appearance: Alert, anxious-appearing male. Speaking in short sentences. Mild diaphoresis.

nodes.title: Initial Assessment
nodes.stem: Gerald, a 65-year-old male with known paroxysmal AF, arrives to the ED with 6 hours of palpitations and progressive dyspnea. He is alert but anxious. HR 148 bpm (irregular), BP 135/85, SpO2 94% on room air, RR 22. He admits he stopped his anticoagulation 3 months ago because he was 'feeling fine'. What is your first priority?

choices.text: Apply supplemental oxygen, establish IV access, and attach cardiac monitor
choices.feedback: Correct. ABCs first. SpO2 94% warrants supplemental O2. IV access and continuous cardiac monitoring are mandatory before any pharmacological intervention. This is haemodynamically stable AF with RVR — you have time to work systematically.

choices.text: Immediately perform DC cardioversion — HR of 148 is dangerous
choices.feedback: Not yet. Gerald is haemodynamically stable — BP 135/85, GCS 15, no signs of shock. Immediate DC cardioversion is reserved for haemodynamically unstable AF (hypotension, acute pulmonary oedema, ischaemic chest pain, or altered consciousness). Rushing to cardioversion here skips critical steps and risks serious harm — he has been off anticoagulation and onset is unclear.

choices.text: Order a 12-lead ECG and wait for results before doing anything else
choices.feedback: The ECG is essential, but do not wait passively. Stabilisation steps — O2, IV access, monitoring — should happen simultaneously. Delay in these basic steps when SpO2 is 94% is not appropriate.

nodes.title: Targeted History
nodes.stem: Gerald is on the monitor, IV is in, and O2 is running at 4L/min via nasal cannula — SpO2 improves to 97%. You now take a focused history. He says the palpitations started suddenly at 8am while having his morning coffee. He has had 3 previous AF episodes, all self-terminating within 12 hours. He denies chest pain, fever, recent illness, or missed medications (other than the anticoagulation). He had a minor stroke 2 years ago with full recovery. Which additional history element is most critical right now?

choices.text: Confirm the exact onset time and ask about any recent alcohol or stimulant use
choices.feedback: Excellent. Onset time is crucial — it determines cardioversion eligibility. Less than 48 hours of confirmed onset allows cardioversion with anticoagulation cover. Greater than 48 hours (or uncertain) requires either TOE-guided cardioversion or 3 weeks of therapeutic anticoagulation first. Alcohol and stimulants (caffeine, cocaine, decongestants) are common AF triggers worth addressing.

choices.text: Ask about his diet and sleep quality
choices.feedback: While lifestyle factors have long-term relevance, they are not the critical piece of history right now. Onset timing and precipitants that affect your acute management plan are the priority.

choices.text: Ask whether he has ever had open heart surgery
choices.feedback: Post-cardiac surgery AF is a known entity, but Gerald's history of paroxysmal AF makes this less relevant acutely. Onset time and stroke/bleeding risk factors are more immediately actionable.

nodes.title: Investigations
nodes.stem: Gerald confirms onset was approximately 6 hours ago — he is confident it started at 8am. He drinks 2–3 standard drinks on weekends. His 12-lead ECG shows: irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, ventricular rate ~148 bpm, narrow QRS complexes, no ST changes or delta waves. You now select investigations. Which combination is most appropriate for initial workup?

choices.text: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TSH, troponin, coagulation studies, chest X-ray
choices.feedback: Correct. This comprehensive screen covers all the key bases: electrolyte disturbance (K⁺, Mg²⁺), thyrotoxicosis (TSH — a reversible and treatable cause), renal function (affects drug dosing and anticoagulation choice), troponin (rule out ACS as trigger), coagulation (baseline before anticoagulation), and CXR (pulmonary oedema, cardiac size). The ECG has confirmed AF — no delta waves rules out WPW.

choices.text: ECG only — you already have the diagnosis
choices.feedback: The ECG confirms AF, but investigations serve multiple purposes: identifying reversible causes (thyrotoxicosis, electrolyte disturbance), guiding drug choice, and establishing baseline before anticoagulation. Skipping them exposes the patient to avoidable risk.

choices.text: Echo and cardiac MRI urgently
choices.feedback: An echo has a role in AF — particularly if structural heart disease is suspected or to guide cardioversion — but it is not a first-line urgent investigation in stable AF with RVR. MRI is not part of the acute workup. Start with blood tests and CXR.

nodes.title: Rate Control Decision
nodes.stem: Investigations are pending. Gerald remains haemodynamically stable with HR 148, BP 135/85. QRS is narrow. No signs of pre-excitation (no delta waves, no short PR) on the ECG. He is symptomatic with dyspnea and palpitations. You decide to initiate rate control. Which agent is most appropriate as first-line?

choices.text: Metoprolol IV 2.5–5mg over 2 minutes (may repeat up to 3 doses)
choices.feedback: Correct. IV beta-blockers are first-line for rate control in AF with RVR when there is no decompensated heart failure or significant reactive airway disease. Metoprolol slows AV nodal conduction effectively. Monitor BP and HR closely. Target resting HR <110 bpm acutely (strict rate control <80 bpm is a longer-term goal).

choices.text: Diltiazem IV 0.25mg/kg over 2 minutes
choices.feedback: Also acceptable. IV diltiazem (non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker) is an effective first-line rate control agent in AF without pre-excitation and without decompensated LV dysfunction. It is equally guideline-recommended alongside beta-blockers. Caution: avoid in known severe LV dysfunction or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

choices.text: Digoxin IV 0.5mg loading dose
choices.feedback: Digoxin is not first-line for acute rate control in AF with RVR in the ED. Its onset of action is slow (2–6 hours for IV), it is less effective during adrenergic states (which is exactly the situation in acute AF), and it has a narrow therapeutic index. It may have a role as adjunctive therapy in heart failure patients, but beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine CCBs should be used first.

choices.text: Diltiazem IV — the ECG shows a delta wave so standard agents are contraindicated
choices.feedback: Careful — the ECG explicitly shows NO delta waves and a normal PR interval. This rules out WPW/pre-excitation. In AF with pre-excitation, AV nodal blocking agents (diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin, adenosine) are contraindicated as they can accelerate accessory pathway conduction and precipitate VF. But that concern does not apply here. Diltiazem is appropriate in this case.

nodes.title: Anticoagulation Assessment
nodes.stem: Gerald's HR has come down to 96 bpm after rate control. He is more comfortable. You now turn to anticoagulation. He was previously on warfarin but stopped 3 months ago. His history includes: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and a prior stroke (full recovery). What is the next step?

choices.text: Calculate his CHA2DS2-VASc score and use it to guide anticoagulation decision
choices.feedback: Exactly right. The CHA2DS2-VASc score is the validated tool for stroke risk stratification in non-valvular AF. Gerald's score: Hypertension (+1), Age 65–74 (+1), Diabetes (+1), Prior stroke/TIA (+2) = total 5. Score ≥2 in males: anticoagulation is strongly recommended. He should be restarted on therapeutic anticoagulation — and this is also critical before any cardioversion attempt.

choices.text: Skip anticoagulation — he had a stroke before and is at bleeding risk
choices.feedback: Incorrect reasoning. A prior stroke significantly INCREASES stroke risk in AF — it contributes 2 points to CHA2DS2-VASc. Unless there is a specific contraindication to anticoagulation (active major bleeding, high-risk haemorrhagic lesion), prior stroke mandates anticoagulation, not the reverse. Always assess bleeding risk separately using HAS-BLED, but do not conflate stroke risk with bleeding risk.

choices.text: Restart his warfarin at his previous maintenance dose — no need to reassess risk
choices.feedback: A new anticoagulation decision should always include a formal risk reassessment — CHA2DS2-VASc for stroke risk and HAS-BLED for bleeding risk. Also, warfarin is not the only option: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are now preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF due to superior safety and efficacy profiles. Additionally, warfarin takes days to become therapeutic — bridging considerations apply.

nodes.title: Cardioversion Decision
nodes.stem: Gerald's CHA2DS2-VASc score is 5. Anticoagulation has been discussed and agreed upon. His onset is confirmed at 6 hours ago (under 48 hours). He remains in AF despite rate control. His symptoms are much improved. The team is now discussing cardioversion. He asks: 'Can you shock my heart back to normal today?' What is the safest approach?

choices.text: Electrical cardioversion is possible given onset <48 hours, but anticoagulation must be started first and adequate rate control confirmed. Discuss risks and obtain consent.
choices.feedback: Correct. For AF of confirmed onset <48 hours, cardioversion can be performed with anticoagulation cover — either therapeutic anticoagulation already established, or heparin administered before cardioversion. Given his high CHA2DS2-VASc score (5), anticoagulation is mandatory both for the cardioversion itself and for long-term maintenance. Post-cardioversion 'stunning' of the atria means embolic risk persists for 4 weeks regardless of apparent sinus rhythm, so therapeutic anticoagulation must continue.

choices.text: Perform DC cardioversion immediately — onset is under 48 hours so it is safe without anticoagulation
choices.feedback: Dangerous. Even with onset <48 hours, cardioversion without any anticoagulation is not safe — particularly in a patient with a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 5. The <48 hour window reduces (but does not eliminate) thrombus risk. Heparin or established therapeutic DOAC levels are required before cardioversion. Skipping this step risks precipitating a cardioembolic stroke.

choices.text: Cardioversion is contraindicated — onset is unknown so the patient must have 3 weeks of anticoagulation first
choices.feedback: Not quite. The 3-week anticoagulation rule applies when onset is >48 hours OR uncertain. Gerald has clearly confirmed his onset was 6 hours ago — this is not an uncertain case. With onset <48 hours and anticoagulation cover, cardioversion is appropriate. Alternatively, a TOE to exclude left atrial thrombus could facilitate earlier cardioversion in uncertain cases.

nodes.title: Disposition and Monitoring Plan
nodes.stem: After rate control and discussion with the cardiology team, a plan is made: commence apixaban (DOAC), admit for monitoring, and plan elective electrical cardioversion in the morning after ensuring therapeutic anticoagulation. Gerald's HR is now 88 bpm, BP 130/80, SpO2 98% on room air. He asks: 'What happens next and what do I need to watch for?' Which discharge/monitoring plan is most appropriate IF he were to be managed as an outpatient (he is medically stable, symptoms resolved, AF persists)?

choices.text: Admit for monitoring given high stroke risk, newly initiated anticoagulation, and planned cardioversion
choices.feedback: Correct for this patient. With a CHA2DS2-VASc of 5, new anticoagulation initiation, and planned cardioversion, inpatient monitoring is appropriate. Outpatient management of stable AF with RVR can be considered in lower-risk patients with established anticoagulation, good follow-up, and no haemodynamic compromise — but Gerald's risk profile warrants admission. Before discharge at any point, ensure clear written instructions on: anticoagulation adherence, AF triggers (alcohol reduction, caffeine, sleep hygiene), and when to return (syncope, chest pain, worsening dyspnea).

choices.text: Discharge home with rate control medications and GP follow-up next week
choices.feedback: Not appropriate here. Gerald has a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 5, newly restarted anticoagulation, and planned cardioversion — this warrants inpatient monitoring. For lower-risk, well-anticoagulated patients with stable AF, outpatient cardioversion and follow-up can be considered, but this patient's profile does not meet that threshold.

choices.text: Transfer to ICU for haemodynamic monitoring
choices.feedback: ICU is not warranted. Gerald is haemodynamically stable with a good response to rate control. ICU-level monitoring is appropriate for unstable AF with refractory haemodynamic compromise, not for a patient with HR 88 and BP 130/80. Cardiac monitored ward bed is appropriate.

diseaseMap.title: Atrial Fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Response
diseaseMap.overview: AF is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, characterised by disorganised atrial electrical activity and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. RVR (HR >100 bpm) occurs when the AV node conducts rapid and irregular atrial impulses. AF with RVR causes haemodynamic compromise through loss of atrial kick, reduced diastolic filling time, and increased myocardial oxygen demand.
diseaseMap.pathophysiology: Multiple re-entrant wavelets in the atria produce chaotic depolarisation. The AV node acts as a gatekeeper — its refractory period limits ventricular rate, but in catecholamine-rich states or with fast-conducting accessory pathways, rates can exceed 150–200 bpm. Atrial stasis, particularly in the left atrial appendage, promotes thrombus formation, increasing stroke risk.
ecgFeatures: Irregularly irregular rhythm (no two R-R intervals identical)
Absent P waves — replaced by fibrillatory baseline (best seen in V1)
Narrow QRS complex (unless aberrant conduction or bundle branch block)
Variable ventricular rate — often 100–180 bpm in RVR

acuteManagement.haemodynamicallyUnstable: Immediate synchronised DC cardioversion (100–200J biphasic). Do not delay for anticoagulation.
haemodynamicallyStable: Step 1: Stabilise — O2, IV access, cardiac monitor
Step 2: Rate control — IV metoprolol or diltiazem (avoid in LV dysfunction or WPW)
Step 3: Investigate — ECG, FBC, UEC, TSH, troponin, coagulation, CXR
Step 4: Stroke risk — calculate CHA2DS2-VASc, initiate anticoagulation if indicated
Step 5: Cardioversion decision — based on onset timing and anticoagulation status

pitfalls: DC cardioversion without anticoagulation when onset is uncertain or >48 hours
Using digoxin as first-line rate control in acute RVR — slow onset, ineffective in high adrenergic states
AV nodal blocking agents in AF with WPW/pre-excitation — can cause VF
Assuming <48 hours onset means anticoagulation is unnecessary before cardioversion
Forgetting post-cardioversion stunning — anticoagulation must continue for minimum 4 weeks after cardioversion regardless of apparent sinus rhythm
longTermManagement: Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF
Rate vs rhythm control strategy decision
Treat underlying precipitants (thyrotoxicosis, alcohol, infection, ischaemia)
Catheter ablation: consider for paroxysmal AF refractory to medical management
Regular review of stroke and bleeding risk scores

guideline.name: CHA2DS2-VASc Stroke Risk Score
guideline.source: ESC 2020 AF Guidelines / AHA/ACC/HRS 2023 AF Guidelines
guideline.description: Validated risk stratification tool for stroke risk in non-valvular atrial fibrillation. Used to guide anticoagulation decisions.

fields.label: Vascular Disease (prior MI, peripheral arterial disease, or aortic plaque)

interpretation.recommendation: Anticoagulation not recommended. Consider no therapy.

interpretation.risk: Low-moderate (males) / Low (females — female sex alone does not warrant anticoagulation)
interpretation.recommendation: Consider anticoagulation based on individual risk-benefit assessment.
Oral anticoagulation recommended. DOAC preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF.

notes: Female sex alone (score of 1 in a female patient with no other risk factors) does not automatically warrant anticoagulation — reassess with other risk factors.
HAS-BLED should be calculated alongside CHA2DS2-VASc to assess bleeding risk — a high HAS-BLED score does not contraindicate anticoagulation but prompts addressing modifiable bleeding risks.
DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF due to superior net clinical benefit.

patientExample.name: Gerald Marsh (this case)
patientExample.interpretation: Score 5 — High risk. Oral anticoagulation strongly recommended. DOAC preferred.

availableBadges.title: Systematic Approach
availableBadges.description: Correctly stabilised the patient before investigation and treatment

availableBadges.title: Rate Controller
availableBadges.description: Selected an appropriate first-line rate control agent
availableBadges.criteria: Chose metoprolol or diltiazem in node-rate-control

availableBadges.title: Stroke Strategist
availableBadges.description: Used CHA2DS2-VASc to guide anticoagulation decision

availableBadges.title: Cardioversion Sage
availableBadges.description: Made the correct cardioversion decision based on onset timing and anticoagulation status

availableBadges.title: Clean Run
availableBadges.description: Completed the case with no incorrect choices
availableBadges.criteria: Zero wrong answers across all nodes

availableBadges.title: First, Do No Harm
availableBadges.description: Avoided all dangerous pitfalls — no DC cardioversion without anticoagulation, no digoxin first-line, no AV nodal block in WPW
availableBadges.criteria: Did not select c1b, c4c, or c6b

disclaimer: This case is intended for educational purposes only. Clinical decisions in real patients must be made by qualified healthcare professionals with full access to the clinical context, local guidelines, and individual patient factors. Drug doses, protocols, and guidelines vary by institution and jurisdiction — always refer to local formularies and current guidelines. This simulation simplifies complex clinical scenarios for learning; real patients present with greater nuance and uncertainty.
